structure.title: Contributors
structure.summary: This partial document is a title page or introductory section for a California Native Plant List, emphasizing the goal of sustaining native plant communities. It also lists the key contributors and organizations involved in its creation or support, including the California Native Plant Society, bewaterwise.com, Armstrong Garden Centers, Theodore Payne, and the County of San Diego.

structure.title: SUGGESTED PLANT LIST FOR A DEFENSIBLE SPACE

nodes.title: TREES
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a suggested plant list, specifically focusing on trees, for creating a "defensible space." It lists various trees by their botanical and common names, along with their recommended climate zones.

nodes.title: SHRUBS
nodes.summary: This partial document is a comprehensive list of various shrubs, organized alphabetically by their scientific names (genus and species). For each shrub, it provides both its scientific and common names, along with a coded classification (e.g., D, I, C, M, R, All Zones) which likely indicates characteristics such as drought tolerance, invasiveness, native status, or suitability for specific conditions or zones. Some entries also feature asterisks, possibly denoting additional specific attributes or notes.

nodes.title: GROUNDCOVERS
nodes.summary: This partial document is a list of groundcover plants. It provides the scientific names for various groundcover species, often accompanied by their common names. Additionally, each entry includes a code or designation (e.g., "All Zones," "C," "C/I/D," "M"), which likely indicates suitability, growing conditions, or specific characteristics for each plant.

nodes.title: VINES
nodes.summary: The partial document provides a categorized list of plants, specifically VINES, PERENNIALS, and ANNUALS. For each plant, it includes both its scientific (botanical) name and common name, along with codes that likely indicate characteristics such as suitability for different zones (e.g., "All Zones"), environmental conditions (e.g., Coastal/Inland, Drought), or maintenance requirements.

nodes.title: PERENNIALS
nodes.summary: The partial document is a categorized list of plants, divided into VINES, PERENNIALS, and ANNUALS. For each plant entry, it provides its scientific name, common name, and a set of codes (e.g., C/I, All Zones, C/I/D, C/M) which likely indicate growing conditions, suitability for different zones (e.g., coastal/inland), or other characteristics.

nodes.title: ANNUALS
nodes.summary: The partial document provides a categorized list of plants, specifically VINES, PERENNIALS, and ANNUALS. For each plant, it includes both its scientific (botanical) name and common name, along with codes that likely indicate characteristics such as suitability for different zones (e.g., "All Zones"), environmental conditions (e.g., Coastal/Inland, Drought), or maintenance requirements.

structure.summary: This partial document presents a suggested list of trees suitable for creating a "defensible space." It is organized with columns for the botanical name, common name, and climate zone for each tree. The list includes a variety of tree species, such as maples, alders, palms, dogwoods, and pistaches.

structure.title: Fire Resistant Native Plants with High Wildlife Value

nodes.title: GROUNDCOVERS
nodes.summary: This partial document, compiled by The Theodore Payne Foundation, provides a list of Californian native plants that are both fire resistant and offer high wildlife value. It clarifies that no plant is fireproof, but "fire resistant" plants retain moisture. The list is designed to help identify suitable plants for Fuel Modification Zones, particularly in areas where brush clearance can leave land barren, and many are approved by Los Angeles County Fire. The document categorizes plants into groundcovers and shrubs, detailing each entry with its scientific and common name, growth characteristics, specific wildlife benefits (e.g., food, cover, nesting), and other ecological advantages like erosion control.

nodes.title: SHRUBS
nodes.summary: This partial document, compiled by The Theodore Payne Foundation, provides a list of fire-resistant native Californian plants that also offer significant wildlife value. It clarifies that while no plant is fireproof, certain native species retain moisture and are considered fire-resistant. The list is specifically designed to aid in planting in Fuel Modification Zones, where brush clearance often leaves areas barren, and many of the listed plants are approved by Los Angeles County Fire. The document categorizes plants into groundcovers and shrubs, detailing each entry with its common and scientific names, growth characteristics, specific wildlife benefits (such as food, cover, and nesting resources), and other advantages like erosion control.

nodes.title: TREES
nodes.summary: This partial document describes two specific tree species, Heteromeles arbutifolia (Toyon) and Quercus agrifolia (Coastal Live Oak), detailing their characteristics, locations, and ecological benefits such as drought tolerance, providing food for wildlife, and fire suppression.

structure.title: Fire-Resistant Plant Guide

nodes.title: Fire-Resistant Trees
nodes.summary: This partial document is a "Fire-Resistant Plant Guide" from Armstrong Garden Centers, focusing on identifying plants with fire retardant abilities for firescaping. It provides extensive lists of recommended fire-resistant trees and shrubs, along with the beginning of a list for fire-resistant ground cover. The guide emphasizes careful plant selection, particularly for trees, due to their long-term presence.

The guide emphasizes careful plant selection, particularly for trees, due to their long-term presence.

structure.title: Fire-Resistant Plant List

nodes.title: Shrubs

It specifically details various fire-resistant shrubs, providing their common and scientific names, physical descriptions, growth habits, drought tolerance, and specific endorsements or recommendations from various California fire departments (e.g., San Diego County, Los Angeles County, Orange County) and other organizations. The document also indicates that it will cover trees, perennials, annuals, and groundcovers in other sections.

nodes.title: Trees
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a categorized list of fire-resistant plants, including shrubs, trees, perennials, annuals, and groundcovers. For each plant, it offers a brief description of its characteristics, appearance, and often its drought tolerance or native status. A key feature is the inclusion of endorsements and approvals from various Southern California fire departments (such as Los Angeles, Orange, and San Diego counties) and other relevant organizations, indicating their suitability for fire-safe landscaping.

nodes.title: Perennials and Annuals

It categorizes plants into types such as trees, perennials/annuals, and groundcover, offering brief descriptions for each. Notably, it highlights specific plants like Coast Live Oak, California Sycamore, Toyon, California Fuchsia, Beard Tongue, and Wild Strawberry, often mentioning their endorsement or approval by various Southern California fire departments and counties (e.g., Los Angeles, Orange, San Diego, Inland Empire).

structure.summary: This partial document is a "Fire-Resistant Plant List" that specifically details various shrubs suitable for fire-prone areas, particularly in California. For each listed shrub, it provides a description of its appearance, growth habits, and care requirements, along with information about its endorsement for fire-resistant landscaping by various California fire departments, county planners, and plant societies. It also frequently highlights the drought-tolerant nature of these plants. The list includes California Redbud, Monkeyflower, Ceanothus ‘Concha’, Sage, Common Yarrow, and French Lavender, and indicates a transition to listing trees.

structure.title: Can You Say... “PYROPHYTIC”

nodes.title: Pyrophytic Characteristics
nodes.summary: This partial document is a "Firewise Planting Guide" for the Big Bear Lake area, created in cooperation with the Sierra Club, aiming to promote a fire-safe community. It primarily focuses on educating readers about "pyrophytic" plants, which are easily ignitable and burn intensely. The guide details the characteristics of high pyrophytic plants (e.g., blade-leaf evergreens, volatile oils, gummy sap, fine twiggy material) and provides specific recommendations for fire-resistant landscaping. Key advice includes planting high pyrophytic plants more than 15 feet from structures, maintaining them by removing dead material, and proper spacing. It also introduces the crucial concept of "Defensible Space" around homes for firefighter safety and describes a "Firewise Landscape" as one that utilizes low pyrophytic plants, strategic placement, and consistent maintenance to resist fire spread and prevent ladder fuels.

nodes.title: Understanding Pyrophytic
nodes.summary: This partial document is a "Firewise Planting Guide" for the Big Bear Lake area, created in cooperation with the Sierra Club, aiming to promote a fire-safe community. It primarily focuses on educating readers about "pyrophytic" plants, which are easily ignitable and burn intensely. The guide details the characteristics of high pyrophytic plants (e.g., blade-leaf evergreens, volatile oils, gummy sap, fine twiggy material) and provides specific recommendations for fire-resistant landscaping. Key advice includes planting high pyrophytic plants more than 15 feet from structures, maintaining them by removing dead material, and proper spacing. It also introduces the crucial concept of "Defensible Space" around homes for firefighter safety and describes a "Firewise Landscape" as one that utilizes low pyrophytic plants, strategic placement, and consistent maintenance to resist fire spread and prevent ladder fuels.

nodes.title: Defensible Space
nodes.summary:
This partial document is a "Firewise Planting Guide" for the Big Bear Lake area, created by the City of Big Bear Lake Fire Department in cooperation with the Sierra Club. Its main purpose is to educate residents on how to create "defensible space" and a "firewise landscape" around their homes to protect against wildfires.

Key points covered include:
*   **Definition and Characteristics of Pyrophytic Plants:** It explains what "pyrophytic" means, particularly "HIGH PYROPHYTIC" plants, which ignite easily and burn intensely. It lists specific characteristics such as blade-leaf/needle-leaf evergreens, volatile oils, gummy sap, and fine, twiggy materials.
*   **Understanding Pyrophytic Properties:** It emphasizes that understanding these properties helps in selecting and placing plants to make landscapes more fire-resistant.
*   **Recommendations for High Pyrophytic Plants:** These plants should be planted more than 15 feet from structures, require removal of dead material, and specific maintenance like raising shrub crowns and proper spacing.
*   **Defensible Space:** The document defines defensible space as the area around a home free from dangerous flammable vegetation, crucial for firefighter safety.
*   **Firewise Landscape:** It describes a firewise landscape as one using "LOW pyrophytic" plants, strategically placed and maintained to resist fire spread, free of dead materials, and properly spaced to prevent "ladder fuels."
*   **Plant Lists:** The guide provides lists of flowering perennials, shrubs, and trees common in the Big Bear Valley, categorizing them as either low or high in pyrophytic properties. High pyrophytic plants are specifically highlighted in red bold italic typeface with an icon.
*   **Overall Goal:** To work together to make the Big Bear Valley a more fire-safe, firewise community.

nodes.title: Flowering Perennials
nodes.summary: This partial document provides lists of Xeriscape and native plants, shrubs, and trees found in the Big Bear Valley. It categorizes these plants based on their "pyrophytic properties," indicating how easily they ignite or burn intensely. The document highlights plants with high pyrophytic properties using a specific typeface and icon, and offers crucial recommendations for their placement and maintenance, such as planting them more than 15 feet from structures, removing dead material, and specific spacing guidelines, all aimed at reducing fire risk.

nodes.title: Trees
nodes.summary: This partial document provides lists of Xeriscape and native plants, shrubs, and trees found in the Big Bear Valley. structure.summary:
This partial document is a "Firewise Planting Guide" for Big Bear, created by the City of Big Bear Lake Fire Department in cooperation with the Sierra Club, aimed at making the community more fire-safe. It focuses on educating readers about "pyrophytic" plants, which are highly flammable and burn intensely.

The main points covered include:
*   **Characteristics of High Pyrophytic Plants:** A detailed list of features that make plants easily ignitable, such as blade/needle-leaves, volatile oils/waxes, gummy sap, fine twiggy material, and papery bark.
*   **Understanding Pyrophytic Properties:** Explaining that plants with high pyrophytic properties ignite easily and burn intensely.
*   **Firewise Landscaping Guidance:** Recommendations for plant selection and placement, advising that high pyrophytic plants should be planted more than 15 feet from structures and properly maintained (removing dead material, raising shrub crowns, appropriate spacing).
*   **Defensible Space:** Defining defensible space as the area around a home free from dangerous flammable vegetation, crucial for firefighter safety and home protection during wildfires.
*   **Low Pyrophytic Plants:** Promoting the use of low pyrophytic plants (less likely to burn) in firewise landscapes, along with proper spacing and maintenance to prevent them from acting as "ladder fuels."
*   **Community Cooperation:** Emphasizing working together to create a fire-safe community.

structure.title: CNPS San Diego: Sources for Native Plants

nodes.title: Sources for Native Plants in Southern California
Annual Plant Sales
nodes.summary: This partial document from CNPS San Diego serves as a guide to finding California native plants, seeds, and bulbs in Southern California, with a particular emphasis on the San Diego area. It outlines various sources, including annual plant sales, monthly meetings of the CNPS San Diego Chapter, and a detailed list of specialty nurseries. The document also advises caution when purchasing from non-specialty nurseries, recommending verification through resources like Cal Flora or the San Diego Plant Atlas Project. It provides specific information for several nurseries, including their locations, contact details, and available plant selections or services.

nodes.title: Sources for California Native Seeds and Bulbs
nodes.summary: This partial document serves as a guide to finding California native plants, seeds, and bulbs, primarily focusing on the Southern California and San Diego areas. It lists various sources, including annual plant sales, suppliers for seeds and bulbs, and the CNPS San Diego Chapter's monthly meetings. A significant portion is dedicated to detailing specialty nurseries in the San Diego region that stock California native species, providing their contact information, locations, and descriptions of their offerings. The document also advises readers to verify native plant lists from non-specialty nurseries using online resources and encourages contributions of information about additional nurseries.

nodes.title: Nurseries in the San Diego Area that Specialize in California Native Species
nodes.summary:
This partial document, provided by the CNPS San Diego Chapter, serves as a comprehensive guide to sources for California native plants in Southern California, with a particular focus on the San Diego area. It lists various avenues for obtaining native plants, including annual plant sales, sources for native seeds and bulbs, and a small selection available at CNPS San Diego Chapter monthly meetings.

The main body of the document is dedicated to an extensive directory of nurseries, botanical gardens, and foundations that specialize in California native species. Each entry typically includes the organization's name, address, phone number, and a brief description of their offerings, such as the types of plants available, whether they offer wholesale or retail, special order capabilities, contract growing, and specific contact persons. The document also provides a cautionary note about verifying species accuracy from non-specialty nurseries and suggests resources like Cal Flora and the San Diego Plant Atlas Project for verification. It encourages readers to submit information about additional nurseries to Kay Stewart.
